coredata.dc:title: To lose is to gain: Effective synthesis of water-soluble graphene fluoroxide quantum dots by sacrificing certain fluorine atoms from exfoliated fluorinated graphene
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Peiwei
Yang, Zhigang
Hong, Wei
Wang, Zhaofeng
Hou, Kaiming
Wang, Jinqing
Yang, Shengrong
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble and blue luminescent graphene fluoroxide quantum dots (GFOQDs) with tunable fluorine coverage and size were effectively synthesized from exfoliated fluorinated graphene (FG) by sacrificing certain fluorine to improved solubility and reaction activity. Morphology investigation indicates that the obtained GFOQDs possess narrow size distribution and the average size is 2.5–3.5nm. Chemical composition analysis confirms that besides C–F covalent bonds, C–O bonds in the forms of hydroxyl and carbonyl co-exist on the structure of GFOQDs. Moreover, photoluminescence performance research considering the surface state and size has also been conducted, and as anticipated in carbon-based quantum dots the GFOQDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent properties. Additionally, rather different from other graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that are often susceptible to pH without additional surface passivation, the GFOQDs themselves are poised to resist pH effects and display stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions. These results indicate that our method not only opens up a new avenue to prepare GQDs decorated with fluorine and oxygen, but also can find practical applications in novel GQDs-based devices that require water solubility while keep chemical stability and resistance against pH.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of FG and activated FG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a) and the EDX spectrum (c) of activated FG-1; (b and d) show the corresponding results for activated FG-2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of the obtained GFOQDs samples: (a) a wide-field image of the obtained GFOQDs-1; (b) a closer look at the size and shape of GFOQDs-1; (c) size distribution of GFOQDs-1; (d–f) show the corresponding experimental results for GFOQDs-2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Chemical composition analyses of the two samples. (a) FTIR spectra and (b) XPS survey spectra for GFOQDs-1 and GFOQDs-2. The chemical components of the GFOQDs samples are labeled; high resolution XPS spectra of C1s for (c) GFOQDs-1 and (d) GFOQDs-2. The insets show the corresponding F1s spectra results for the two samples. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Molecular models of the activation and cutting processes. The proposed activated sites on FG sheets after being processed by mixed alkali are painted pink. For clarity, hydrogen atoms on FG or GFOQDs are not shown. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical properties of samples: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum for GFOQDs-1. The two inset images show GFOQDs-1 dispersed in water before and after being excited by a UV beam of 365nm. (b) Room temperature PL and PLE spectra for GFOQDs-1; (c) PL spectra of GFOQDs-1 at excitation wavelengths varying from 320 to 440nm. (d–f) Displays the corresponding experimental data for GFOQDs-2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Possible luminescence emission mechanism and electronic transition for the samples. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

pH effect on the PL behavior for samples of GFOQDs-1 and GFOQDs-2. (a) PL intensity of GFOQDs-1 accompanying with different pH values; (b) the shift of emission peaks of GFOQDs-1 as a function of different pH values; (c) and (d) show the corresponding experimental data for GFOQDs-2. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Location and ascription of chemical groups of the two GFOQDs obtained from XPS investigation.

head.ce:title: To lose is to gain: Effective synthesis of water-soluble graphene fluoroxide quantum dots by sacrificing certain fluorine atoms from exfoliated fluorinated graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble and blue luminescent graphene fluoroxide quantum dots (GFOQDs) with tunable fluorine coverage and size were effectively synthesized from exfoliated fluorinated graphene (FG) by sacrificing certain fluorine to improved solubility and reaction activity. Morphology investigation indicates that the obtained GFOQDs possess narrow size distribution and the average size is 2.5–3.5nm. Chemical composition analysis confirms that besides C–F covalent bonds, C–O bonds in the forms of hydroxyl and carbonyl co-exist on the structure of GFOQDs. Moreover, photoluminescence performance research considering the surface state and size has also been conducted, and as anticipated in carbon-based quantum dots the GFOQDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent properties. Additionally, rather different from other graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that are often susceptible to pH without additional surface passivation, the GFOQDs themselves are poised to resist pH effects and display stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions. These results indicate that our method not only opens up a new avenue to prepare GQDs decorated with fluorine and oxygen, but also can find practical applications in novel GQDs-based devices that require water solubility while keep chemical stability and resistance against pH.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorinated graphene (FG), a recently discovered and experimentally available two-dimensional carbon material, has been attracting increasingly intense attention among engineers and scientists due to its novel and noble properties [1–5]. What makes it a more alluring material is its rising fluorescent performance; compared to traditional luminescent materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, silicon nanoparticles, and gold nanodots [6], it has broader optical absorptivity, exhibits more stable emission, enjoys finer biocompatibility and causes less environment concern [7–9]. Yet like its other two-dimensional cousins, relatively lower fluorescent intensity makes it difficult to be directly employed as a fluorescent material [7,8,10]. Thus to fully display FG’s properties and explore its potential applications, a reliable and effective method to improve the fluorescent intensity of FG while keeping its unique structure and properties is of particular importance.

Reducing the size of graphene down to 10nm or synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with smaller size provides an effective way to manipulate the structural, optical, electronic, and magnetic properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects [11–13]. And specially, strong absorption and photoluminescence (PL) performance can be obtained when the graphene sheets are cut into GQDs. On the other hand, it is found that graft of fluorine onto graphene scaffold can bring exotic optical [7,14], electronic [2,15], magnetic [8,16], electrochemical [17,18] and biological [9,19] properties into the two-dimensional carbon. As for fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), it is reasonable to speculate that much richer phenomena are expected if fluorine atoms are introduced into the zero-dimensional carbon. However, the advent and prosperity of FGQDs are not happening in the way like GQDs, mostly because the chemically inert bonds and hydrophobic nature of FG prevent it from being easily cut into quantum dots [1,20,21]. This further makes it impossible to simply transplant the methods that are previously employed to prepare GQDs into FGQDs, and in fact these methods including bottom-up [22–26] and top-down [27–31] lose their sparkle in the face of FG, both of which raise rigorous challenges for the preparation of FGQDs. Therefore, no matter from the perspective of scientific investigations in chemical labs or for the sake of its further applications in technological field, an effective and facile method to prepare FGQDs is highly desirable.

In this study, a strategy to tackle this dilemma by a facile activating-cutting route is developed. Commercially available fluorinated graphite (FGi) was first exfoliated into few-layered FG sheets in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) according to our previous work [4], and then an exquisite nucleophilic reagent based on a mixture of KOH and NaOH is formulated to activate FG. The formulated alkali can provide free nucleophilic hydroxyl anions to substitute fluorine atoms at temperatures that are far below their individual melting point (higher than 300°C) as well as the decomposition temperature of FG [1,8,32], which is essential to simultaneously keep fluorine atoms on FG while introduce oxygen atoms into FG. Then a modified mixed acid used to generate oxygen groups on carbon materials [30,33,34] is modulated, and the activated FG sheets are oxidated in the mixed acid under sonication and finally they are hydrothermally cut into graphene fluoroxide quantum dots (GFOQDs). By an effective control over the reaction ratio of FG to the mixed alkali, the fluorine coverage on activated FG sheets can be easily controlled and accordingly GFOQDs with tunable fluorine content can be actualized. Additionally, the obtained GFOQDs exhibit bright blue luminescence under UV excitation, and display stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions relying on its own strength without additional surface passivation. And thanks to the introduction of oxygen groups, the obtained GFOQDs are readily dispersed in water or other common solvents, and they are stable in water for months. More importantly, compared to previous work [20], employing FGi as the raw material, our method avoids the complex and time-consuming procedures of preparing graphene oxide (GO), reducing GO and fluorinating the reduced GO by violent and highly toxic gas (such as F2 or XeF2). Thus it is much simpler, more time-saving and secure, guaranteeing the method can be easily realized in a common lab without special equipment for gas protection. More importantly, it is suggested that the loss of certain fluorine atom on FG is not always a bad thing; on the contrary, to lose may mean to gain more opportunities. The interesting achievements and useful experimental results presented here are expected to ignite more enthusiasm for FG, FGQDs, GFOQDs or other graphene related materials, and promote these materials to find more applications in various technological fields.

FGi (Grade II) was purchased from Shanghai CarFluor Ltd. And then it was used without further purification. Elemental investigation by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicates the atomic content of fluorine in FGi is about 45.7%, and a more detailed analysis of the chemical composition of FGi is provided in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary material part. Other chemicals such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), KOH or NaOH used in our experiments were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Milli-Q water (>18MΩcm) was used for rinsing and as the solvent as well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of FG and activated FG
ce:section.ce:para: FG dispersion was prepared by adding FGi into NMP solvent at an initial concentration of C FGi =5mg/mL in a round-bottomed flask, and then the mixed solution was first heated at 60°C for 2h. After cooling down to room temperature, ultrasonication was then carried out in a low power sonic bath (50%, Kunshan KQ-300DE) for 20h. FG was pipeted from the flask, washed with Milli-Q water several times to remove the residual NMP, and finally dried through vacuum freeze for 48h. The morphology of FGi and FG is investigated and provided in Fig. S2 in the Supplementary material part.

According to previous work on the phase equilibria of NaOH and KOH [32], and the stability of graphene oxide and FG [8,35], the eutectic point of the mixed alkali can be tuned around 170°C and the lowest freezing point is at 174.5°C when the weight ratio of NaOH is 43.1%, while oxygen groups on graphene sheets would fast decompose if the temperature is higher than 200°C. Considering all these in mind, the reaction temperature was set at 180°C and the weight ratio of NaOH in the mixed alkali is about 43.1%. Such a consideration meets the acquired temperature to obtain mobile nucleophilic hydroxyl anions, and simultaneously guarantees more oxygen groups remaining on FG sheets. Meanwhile, in order to obtain activated FG sheets with relatively higher fluorine coverage, the weight ratio of FG and the alkali was modulated as the following: for sample of activated FG-1, 100mg FG was reacted with 318.7mg mixed alkali; while for sample of activated FG-2, 100mg FG was reacted with 478.0mg mixed alkali.

After being blended uniformly with the mixed alkali powder, the two samples were heated at 180°C in air for 6h under the same reaction conditions. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were dispersed in 100mL Milli-Q water and sonicated for 1h to dissolve alkali and further exfoliate the obtained FG sheets. After the residual FG that flows on the surface of the solution was carefully pipetted, the product was vacuum filtered, rinsed with Milli-Q water (until the pH value reaches neutral) and dried through vacuum freeze drying for 48h.

To prepare GFOQDs, 50mg activated FG-1 was first dispersed in 10mL concentrated H2SO4 and 3mL Milli-Q water, and then the solution was mildly sonicated for 2h (Kunshan KQ-500DE, 500W, 40kHz). After 30mL concentrated HNO3 was added, the solution was further sonicated for another 20h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was diluted by 250mL water and filtered through a 0.22-μm microporous membrane. Then the product was re-dispersed in 40mL water and the pH value was tuned to 8 with NaOH. The dispersion was heated at 200°C for 10h in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. After cooling down, the dispersion was sonicated for 1h, and filtered through the 0.22-μm microporous membrane. The filtered solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500Da) for 3days, and before characterization or PL investigation, the solution was re-filtered again to remove remaining larger FG sheets after dialysis. The obtained GFOQDs were abbreviated as GFOQDs-1. Accordingly, GFOQDs-2 were prepared and purified under the same conditions.

The morphologies of the synthesized samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOLJEM-2010) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) detection. The chemical composition of the samples was measured by a Fourier transformation infrared spectrometer (FTIR, IFS 66 V/S Bruker, Germany), and XPS (PHI-5702, Physical Electronics, USA) using monochromated Al Kα irradiation with chamber pressure of 3×10−8 Torr under testing conditions (all the binding energies were calibrated using 284.8eV of C 1s). UV–Vis absorbance experiments were carried on UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu). The photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a FLS-920T fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 450W Xe light source and double excitation monochromators.

After being activated, previously water-insoluble FG can be dispersed in water and good dispersibility can be easily achieved after mild sonication (Supplementary material, see the digital images of FG, activated FG, acid treated FG and GFOQDs dispersed in water in Fig. S3). Fig. 1 a shows a typical TEM image of activated FG-1, exhibiting a large and transparent sheet with edges freely covering several pores on the Cu grids. And TEM image of activated FG-2 in Fig. 1b displays a corrugated surface of the obtained FG sheets. The corresponding chemical composition analyses from EDX indicate that besides fluorine and carbon elements, oxygen element can also be found in the two samples, showing the effectiveness of introduction of oxygen and reservation of fluorine atoms on the scaffold of FG. On the other hand, it is worth noticing that previous researchers conducted the reaction of alkali with FGi [36], and it was found that no detectable fluorine remained and FGi sheets became smaller. A careful investigation on this work indicates that high weight ratio of alkali to FGi (43.6/1) and high reaction temperature (250°C) might not only destroy the structure of FGi, but also induce complete loss of fluorine. According to all the above experimental data and discussion, it is suggested that the carefully pre-designed reaction conditions in our work allow a finer control over the fluorine content and less destruction of FG sheet. Meanwhile, according to our preliminary experiments, it is observed that although directly adding mixed acid into activated FG like the way to prepare GQDs [30] can also obtain GFOQDs, yet the yield is found to be low. It is considered that although FG has been activated, it is still hard to be oxidized as easy as reduced graphene oxide. Inspired by the preparation process of graphene oxide [35], a pre-oxidized process using diluted H2SO4 was first introduced and then FG was further oxidized in mixed acid as described in Section 2 to prepare GFOQDs.

A definitive quality identification of the prepared GFOQDs was first made by TEM characterizations to investigate their morphology. It is clearly seen in Fig. 2 a that GFOQDs-1 with relatively uniform size is successfully obtained, and a closer look at the quantum dots in the magnified TEM image in Fig. 2b indicates the average lateral dimension of the sample is about 3.5nm. Meanwhile, statistic size distribution in Fig. 2c shows the size of the GFOQDs-1 mostly (about 70%) falls within the scope of 3–4nm. As for the morphology of GFOQDs-2, corresponding experimental results are displayed in Fig. 2d–f. It is noticed that the average size of GFOQDs-2 is about 2.5nm and most sizes of the samples (about 75%) are in the range of 2–3nm, which is smaller than that of FGQD-1. Moreover, it is found that larger ones (like 5.5nm and 6nm) can be found in GFOQDs-1, while few can be observed in GFOQDs-2. It seems that under the same reaction conditions, activated FG-2 tends to be more readily cut than activated FG-1. As analyzed from EDX spectra in Fig. 1, as well as from quantitative content analyses in Table S1 in the Supplementary material, the fluorine content of activated FG-1 is higher than that of FG-2. This interesting experimental data suggests that the more fluorine atoms the FG sheet contains, the harder it would be cut. Taking the experimental fact that FG without activation is hard to be cut in consideration, it is reasonable to speculate that FG with more fluorine atoms might be more inert than that with less ones. Accordingly, there is no doubt that the mixed acid and following hydrothermal cutting process would choose the low-hanging fruit, causing FG-2 to be more easily cut into smaller ones.

To present clearer element information of the samples, FTIR and XPS measurements were carried out on the obtained samples. FTIR spectra in Fig. 3 a clearly indicate that besides prominently covalent C–F bond (1215cm−1), C–O bond in the forms of C–OH (1384cm−1) or CO (1725cm−1) also can be found on the structure of GFOQDs, both of which form during the oxidization process and guarantee the improved dispersibility of GFOQDs in water. Similar chemical analyses are also reflected by XPS measurements in Fig. 3b–d. XPS survey spectra in Fig. 3b show that while both samples possess similar oxygen contents, the fluorine content of GFOQDs-1 is higher than that of GFOQDs-2. This result agrees well with our initial design to prepare GFOQDs with different fluorine content by tuning the fluorine coverage of the mother FG sheets. Meanwhile, peaks located at 689.2, 834.4 and 864.0eV of the two samples can be indexed as F1s of C–F bond and F Auger [43]. A detailed composition and nature of the chemical bonds of the samples were investigated by high resolution XPS spectra in Fig. 3c and d (the insets show the corresponding F1s spectra for GFOQDs-1 and GFOQDs-2). As for GFOQDs, four fitted peaks centered at about 284.7, 285.8, 286.8 and 288.0eV are corresponding to CC, C–C, C–O, and CO groups [37,38,42,43], respectively; and the peaks centered at about 289.3, 290.2 and 291.3eV are ascribed to weak C–F (w-C–F), covalent C–F and –CF2 groups [15,16,37–41], respectively. Moreover, the F1s spectra of GFOQDs-1 (the inset in Fig. 3c) and GFOQDs-2 (the inset in Fig. 3d) locating at 689.2eV also indicate that covalent C–F bonds exist on the structure of GFOQDs. It is noticed that the experimental data from FTIR and XPS spectra agree well with each other, suggesting that fluorine atoms are successfully kept on GFOQDs while oxygen atoms are also introduced onto the structure.

The fluorine doping level of the GFOQDs samples can be tuned just by pre-treating FG, and a detailed experimental data of location and ascription of the chemical groups of the two GFOQDs obtained from XPS investigation are summarized in Table 1 , and a more detailed investigation on the element contents of the FGi, other inter-mediate products and GFOQDs are provided in Table S1 in the Supplementary material. These above elemental analyses again demonstrate that besides C–F bonds, C–O groups are also successfully grafted onto the small scaffold of GFOQDs, and these data further prove that our method is effective and easy to synthesize GFOQDs by a more simple way, making it possible for GFOQDs with tunable fluorine coverage and uniform size to be prepared from commercially available FGi under these experimental conditions.

According to above analyses and discussions on morphological and elemental data, it is suggested that activation of FG at the sacrifice of certain fluorine atoms is meaningful on the preparation of GFOQDs. Due to the high electronegativity of F and the strong bond energy of C–F bond [44], FG is chemically inert and even stable against the erosion of aqua regia. Loss of certain fluorine atoms not only changes the solubility of FG from insoluble into soluble in water, but also greatly improves the activity of FG. In FGi and FG, the strong C–F bonds not only endow dramatic stability of the two fluorinated materials, but also make it difficult for other functional groups to attack carbon atoms and then modify the carbon structure. Loss of some fluorine atoms makes the carbon scaffold to be exposed to the acid reaction environment and thus facilitates the carbon atoms to be oxidated like carbon nanotubes or expanded graphite [33,34]. So this serves as an activation process, and during the oxidation and sonication process, epoxy groups tend to form a line on the lattice of graphene and the cooperative alignment induces the rupture of the underlying C–C bonds, similar to the preparation of GQDs from reduced graphene oxide or carbon nanotubes [30,31]. This proposed reaction process can also be supported by FTIR and XPS spectra of GFOQDs, in which significant carbonyl groups can be found. This is because these epoxy chains are energetically preferable to be further oxidized into epoxy pairs and then converted into more stable carbonyl groups even at room temperature [30,45]. According to above experimental results, the corresponding reaction process can be schematically summarized in Fig. 4 .

These presented experimental results demonstrate that this method is effective in synthesizing GFOQDs and tuning their surface chemistry by pre-designing the structure of FG, and can be employed as a useful tool in preparing GQDs-based electronic and optical devices. Moreover, one of the most important goals to synthesize GFOQDs is to improve the fluorescence intensity and thus pave the way for its being directly employed as a fluorescent material. Therefore, investigation on the optical performance of the two GFOQDs was carefully conducted. UV–Vis absorption measurement was first carried out as a probe for exploring the optically electronic configuration of the samples. As shown in Fig. 5 a and d, the spectra of two GFOQDs exhibit similar absorption behavior, characteristic with a π–π∗ plasmon peak near 260nm and a shoulder at about 370nm from n–π∗ transitions [25,46]. Meanwhile, the insets in Fig. 5a and d show the aqueous solutions of the two GFOQDs before and after irradiation, and both GFOQDs solutions emit bright blue luminescence under irradiation by a 365nm lamp, displaying greatly improved fluorescence intensity compared to FG sheets (See the fluorescent photos of FG in Fig. S4 in the Supplementary material part).

The similarity of the two GFOQDs in the electronic configuration and emission color indicate that the two samples might enjoy similar PL performance. To have a good understanding of the PL properties of the samples, PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum experiments were carried out on the two samples. The GFOQDs-1 presents a strong peak at 436nm and a shoulder peak at 360nm when excited by 320nm wavelength (Fig. 5b), while GFOQDs-2 exhibits similar emission peaks at 433nm and 360nm (Fig. 5e). Meanwhile, both the PLE spectra of the two samples under the monitor of 436nm and 433nm show strong peaks around 320nm. On the other hand, excitation-dependent PL behavior is commonly found in fluorescent carbon materials [24,25], which affords multi-PL colors under different excitation wavelengths and is potentially important for certain practical applications. Thus the PL behaviors of the two samples under different excitation wavelengths are also investigated and interestingly, both of them exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors when excited from 320nm to 440nm wavelength (Fig. 5c and f). As for GFOQDs-1, the emission peaks vary from 518nm (excited by 440nm) to 436nm (excited by 320nm), while for GFOQDs-2 the emission peaks shift from 520nm (excited by 440nm) to 433nm (excited by 320nm). PL research and experimental data analyses of the two GFOQDs indicate that although the size and chemical composition of the two samples vary from each other, they exhibit very similar PL performance and this indicates that they might share the analogous PL emission mechanism as well.

Previous research concerning on the emission mechanism of GQDs and other carbon based materials pointed out that quantum-confinement effect induced intrinsic emission [22,23], and emission from functional groups like NH2 [48] or from surface defect such as edge sites and other trap states [29,49], might be the origin of PL emission. Thus it is suggested that the true PL mechanism for carbon related materials remains to be explored, and a widely-accepted luminescence mechanism is still awaiting discovery. On the other hand, it is also commonly believed that these materials should enjoy similar or even the same fluorescent origin because they possess similar structure and exhibit similar PL performance such as stable PL emission against photo-bleaching [50,51] and excitation-dependent PL behaviors [24,25]. And the reason for the excitation-dependent phenomena in our experiment is mostly ascribed to emissive traps induced by structural defects formed during preparation of FGi, activated FG or the oxidation-cutting process and surface state changes as a result of fluorine and oxygen doping. This deduction is further supported by both our experimental results and previous mechanism investigation on GQDs. Earlier research on PL behaviors of GQDs pointed out that size polydispersity, free zigzag sites or electronic conjugate structures [24,27,47,52] can contribute to the excitation-dependent performance. In our case, neither distinction of average size distribution (3.5nm for GFOQDs-1 and 2.5nm for GFOQDs-2) nor variation of free zigzag sites or electronic structures (the total F and O contents is different, and either content of them is also different) do anything to the PL behavior of the two different GFOQDs (see the detailed optical property investigation of the two samples in Fig. 5). This means that for the two samples, besides the intrinsic energy level of π and π∗, there exists various emissive traps that generate different discrete energy levels between them, and the energy difference between π and the transition state is about 1.42eV (absorption peaks of 260nm and 370nm). The discrete energy levels thus allow for the existence of a series of emission peaks under different excitation energies, which further leads to colorful PL emissions shifting from about 520nm to 430nm (Fig. 5c and f). According to above experimental results and discussions, a visualized model of this explanation can be schematically summarized in Fig. 6 .

These above investigations on GFOQDs concerning on chemical, morphological and PL performance in the experiment indicate that highly fluorescent GFOQDs with tunable size and fluorine coverage can be readily obtained from commercialized FGi by sacrificing some fluorine. Besides the simple and easy synthesis method, it is considered that due to the strong C–F bond energy and high electronegativity gap between carbon and fluorine atoms [44], the obtained GFOQDs are more chemically stable than other fluorescent GQDs or graphene based materials [37], and thus much richer phenomena are expected from GFOQDs. One of these is that the prepared GFOQDs can easily handle the provocation of pH on its own without any passivation, which previously could only be achieved in GQDs after being decorated with polymers [13]. As displayed in Fig. 7 a and c, once powerful changes of pH can no longer cast much effect on the PL emission intensity of the two GFOQDs, and the PL peak position of the samples also does not vary much accompanying with different pH values (Fig. 7b and d). This PL performance of GFOQDs distinguishes themselves from other graphene or carbon based quantum dots, and it means that robust luminescence can be readily obtained from GFOQDs under different acid or base conditions without any post-treatment.

The mechanism previously employed to explain the effects of pH mostly considers that a protonation and de-protonation process between the zigzag sites and hydrogen ion can affect the PL behavior of GQDs by changing the pH conditions [30], and that passivation of GQDs by removal of functional groups or decoration with polymers can always degrade the influence of pH changes [13]. Considering all these in mind and based on the experimental data in our work, it is no wonder that for GFOQDs fluorine atoms play the key role in the effective resistance of pH change. Yet then the question is how fluorine atoms work out this effect? According to classical Linus Carl Pauling theory on the electronegativity of element, fluorine atom enjoys the highest electronegativity value (4.0 on the Pauling scale) [44]. Thus the high difference value between fluorine and carbon (2.55 on the Pauling scale) [44] makes it easy for the charge of carbon atoms on GFOQDs tending to transfer onto fluorine atoms, and accordingly carbon atoms on the scaffold exhibit more positive charge. These positive carbon atoms on the nano-scaled graphene sheets not only greatly influence the charge distribution of GFOQDs, but also can effectively avoid or degrade the protonation process in acid conditions due to electrostatic repulsion. And this further prevents the transition of carbene state on GFOQDs, which in turn and finally leads to stable emission regardless of pH change [30]. On the other hand, it is also observed that fluorine atoms can also dramatically affect the surface energy of GFOQDs like other fluorine-contained carbon materials [53,54], and this effect also makes it possible for fluorine atoms on GFOQDs play the role of passivation like polymers. Either the changes of charge distribution or surface energy on GFOQDs due to the functionalization of fluorine atoms, or might both of them cooperatively guarantee that GFOQDs can exhibit stable PL emission no matter how the environmental condition changes, and well explain the experimental results in our work. Meanwhile, it should be noticed that although the developed mechanism is reasonable for our experimental results and might also work or provide enlightening insight into other graphene based quantum dots, it is believed that a more precise explanation for this pH dependent PL behavior of graphene or carbon based fluorescent materials is needed, and there is even a long way to go before a commonly accepted and applicable consistency is reached.

In conclusion, this work presents a novel and effective alternative to controllably prepare fluorine and oxygen co-doped GFOQDs with tunable size from exfoliated FG at the sacrifice of some fluorine. By an effective control over the pre-treatment of FG with mixed alkali, once chemically inert FG becomes active and can be tailored into quantum dots by our designed oxidation-cutting routes. And this method allows us to prepare GFOQDs with tunable size and controllable fluorine coverage by pre-designing the elemental components of FG. The obtained GFOQDs possess relatively uniform size, exhibit highly blue luminescence and enjoy good solubility and stability in water. Moreover, the GFOQDs are poised to resist pH effects and display stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions, greatly distinguishing themselves from other common GQDs. And mechanism investigation indicates that the changes of electronic distribution or surface energy due to fluorine invasion account for this novel property. These experimental data and discussions demonstrate that to lose is not always a bad thing and on the other hand it may bring more opportunities. Considering effective tunability of the method, and the outstanding and unique properties of the GFOQDs samples, this work will provide enlightening insights into chemical functionalization and preparation of GQDs, and promote the practical applications of novel GQDs-based devices that require water solubility while keeping chemical stability and resistance against pH.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Professor Yuhua Wang and Dr. Quansheng Wu in Lanzhou University for PL characterization, data collection and analyses. We also thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51205385 and 51375474), and the “Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists of Gansu Province”.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.11.027.
